title: Castile & Aragon
subtitle: 1230–1492 · Union, Reconquest and the Catholic Monarchs

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From Ferdinand III to the Catholic Monarchs — the making of Spain

entries.label: Ferdinand III — the Saint who unified Castile and León
entries.hint: Ferdinand III unites the crowns of Castile and León permanently (1230) — then conquers Córdoba (1236), Jaén (1246) and Seville (1248) in rapid succession; canonised in 1671; his tomb in Seville Cathedral contains his sword and a multilingual epitaph in Latin, Castilian, Arabic and Hebrew.
entries.note: Ferdinand III's multilingual epitaph: Cathedral of Seville — the four languages symbolise his multicultural kingdom. His canonisation 1671: Vatican records. Primera Crónica General (Alfonso X, c.1270): documents his campaigns. The unification of Castile and León 1230: documented in royal charters (AHN). Linehan (1993), History and Historians of Medieval Spain: analyses the source tradition.

entries.label: Alfonso X el Sabio — the scholar king
entries.hint: Alfonso X is the most intellectually productive medieval Iberian monarch — he supervises the Siete Partidas (law code), the Cantigas de Santa María (420 songs with musical notation), astronomical tables, and a general history of Spain; his multilingual court includes Christian, Muslim and Jewish scholars.
entries.note: Alfonso X's reign: documented through his own prolific output. The Siete Partidas: multiple manuscripts; Las siete partidas del Rey don Alfonso (BnF and BNE). The Cantigas de Santa María: 4 illuminated manuscripts survive — Real Biblioteca del Monasterio de El Escorial (T.I.1 and B.I.2), BNE and BnF. His astronomical Alfonsine Tables: used in Europe until the 17th century — British Library holds early copies.

entries.label: Sancho IV -- the rebellious son
entries.hint: Sancho IV usurps the throne from his father Alfonso X's designated heirs (the Infantes de la Cerda), triggering a succession dispute that runs through the next two reigns; his short reign is defined by noble resistance and the ongoing wars over Gibraltar.
entries.note: Cronica de Sancho IV (Pedro Lopez de Ayala): primary chronicle. The Infantes de la Cerda dispute: documented in royal and papal correspondence (AHN).

entries.label: Ferdinand IV -- the minority and noble disorder
entries.hint: Ferdinand IV inherits at age nine under his mother Maria de Molina's regency -- one of medieval Castile's most capable queen-regents, who navigates three competing claims to the throne (the Infantes de la Cerda, the king of Aragon, the king's own uncle); Ferdinand IV dies mysteriously at 26, allegedly 'emplazado' (summoned to divine judgment) by two knights he had executed, giving his reign an enduring legend.
entries.note: Cronica de Fernando IV: primary. Maria de Molina's regency documented in Gomez Redondo (2002). The 'emplazamiento' legend: documented in chronicle sources though its historicity is disputed.

entries.label: Alfonso XI — Río Salado and the Black Death at Gibraltar
entries.hint: Alfonso XI ('the Implacable') defeats the Marinids and Nasrids at Río Salado (1340), permanently ending North African intervention; he consolidates royal power, codifies Castilian law in the Ordenamiento de Alcalá (1348 — the act that finally gives the Siete Partidas legal force); he dies of the Black Death at the Siege of Gibraltar (1350) — the only western European monarch to die of plague.
entries.note: Alfonso XI's reign: Crónica de Alfonso XI (primary). The Battle of Río Salado 30 Oct 1340: ibid. The Ordenamiento de Alcalá 1348: AHN — gives the Siete Partidas legal force. His death at Gibraltar 26 Mar 1350: Crónica de Alfonso XI — documented as the Black Death; confirmed by the timing and symptoms described. O'Callaghan (2011), The Gibraltar Crusade: the standard modern study. He is the only western European monarch documented to have died of plague.

entries.label: Pedro I 'the Cruel' -- civil war and the Trastamara crisis
entries.hint: Pedro I's reign is one long civil war -- against his illegitimate half-brothers (the Trastamara faction, entry k7), the kings of Aragon and France, and much of his own nobility; his alliance with England (the Black Prince fights for him at Najera, 1367) and his enemies' alliance with France bring the Hundred Years' War directly into Iberia; he is killed by his half-brother Henry at Montiel (1369), ending the legitimate Burgundian line and installing the Trastamara dynasty.
entries.note: Cronica del rey don Pedro (Lopez de Ayala) -- primary. Battle of Najera 1367 (Black Prince involvement): English chronicle sources (Froissart). Montiel 1369: cross-reference k7.

entries.label: Henry II -- the Trastamara revolution
entries.hint: Henry of Trastamara's victory over his half-brother Pedro I at the Battle of Montiel (1369, entry c6) installs a new dynasty -- the Trastamara line -- that rules Castile until 1555 and, through the Compromise of Caspe (entry k6), Aragon as well; Henry's victory depends heavily on French and mercenary support (Bertrand du Guesclin's free companies) within the wider Hundred Years' War, and he rewards his noble backers with the 'mercedes enriquenas' -- vast grants of land and jurisdiction that permanently strengthen the high nobility against the crown, reversing a century of royal centralisation.
entries.note: Battle of Montiel 14 Mar 1369 and Pedro I's killing: Cronica del rey don Pedro (Pedro Lopez de Ayala) -- primary chronicle source. The mercedes enriquenas: documented in royal grant registers (AHN, Nobleza) -- the territorial basis of Castile's great noble houses (Velasco, Mendoza, Alburquerque) for the next two centuries. Du Guesclin's role: French chronicle sources confirm the Hundred Years' War dimension. Valdeon Baruque (1966), Enrique II de Castilla: la guerra civil y la consolidacion del regimen (1366-1371): the standard study of the dynastic shift and its noble-empowering consequences.

entries.label: James I of Aragon — the Mediterranean empire
entries.hint: James I of Aragon conquers the Balearic Islands (1229) and Valencia (1238) — establishing the Crown of Aragon as a Mediterranean power; his own autobiography the Llibre dels Fets is the first royal autobiography in a European vernacular language.
entries.note: Llibre dels Fets (Book of Deeds, c.1270–1276): James I's own autobiography — oldest royal autobiography in a vernacular European language. BNE and Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó hold manuscripts. Smith & Buffery (2003), The Book of Deeds of James I of Aragon: the standard English translation. The Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean expansion: documented in treaty archives.

entries.label: Peter III of Aragon -- the Sicilian Vespers king
entries.hint: Peter III accepts the Sicilian crown after the 1282 Vespers uprising (entry c7) -- triggering a war with the Angevins, the Papacy (which declares a crusade against him) and France (which invades Aragon in 1285); he dies the same year defending his kingdom against the French invasion; his nine-year reign transforms Aragon from an Iberian kingdom into the Mediterranean empire documented in entry w3 -- he is the hinge figure of Aragonese history.
entries.note: Peter III's acceptance of Sicily Aug 1282: Archivo de la Corona de Aragon. The Crusade against Aragon (declared by Pope Martin IV): Vatican registers. The French invasion of Catalonia and Peter III's death 1285: Catalan chronicle sources (Muntaner).

entries.label: Alfonso III, James II and Alfonso IV -- consolidating the Mediterranean empire
entries.hint: Three reigns spanning 51 years consolidate Aragon's Mediterranean position: Alfonso III (1285–1291) negotiates the Peace of Tarascon ending the French crusade; James II (1291–1327) adds Sardinia and Corsica to the empire (1324) and presides over the Catalan Company's Athenian adventure (entry w2); Alfonso IV (1327–1336) holds the empire together through renewed conflicts with Genoa over Sardinian trade.
entries.note: Peace of Tarascon 1291: ACA. Sardinian conquest 1324: ACA, documented military campaign. James II's reign as the apex of diplomatic Aragonese imperialism: Hillgarth (1976), The Spanish Kingdoms 1250-1516, vol.1 -- the standard English study of this period.

entries.label: Peter IV -- 'the Ceremonious', and Mallorca's permanent incorporation
entries.hint: Peter IV strengthens royal institutions through an elaborate court ceremonial (earning his epithet) while permanently incorporating the Kingdom of Mallorca into the Crown of Aragon (1343, ending its brief existence as a separate Mallorcan kingdom under a cadet branch) and fighting prolonged wars against Genoa over Sardinia and Corsica; his reign's final years are dominated by the War of the Two Peters (entry c6) against Pedro I of Castile -- the most destructive inter-kingdom conflict of the century, devastating the Aragonese-Valencian frontier.
entries.note: Mallorca's incorporation 1343 (deposing James III of Mallorca): documented in the Cronica of Peter IV (Pere III in Catalan numbering), his own chronicle -- primary, one of the Four Great Catalan Chronicles. The Genoese wars over Sardinia/Corsica: Archivo de la Corona de Aragon. The War of the Two Peters (1356-1369, entry c6): the dominant conflict of his later reign. Ferrer i Mallol (1987), Entre la paz y la guerra: la Corona catalano-aragonesa y Castilla en la baja edad media: the standard study of Peter IV's wars with Castile.

entries.label: John I and Martin I -- the last direct Aragonese line
entries.hint: John I (1387–1396) is a cultured, French-influenced king who patronises music and letters but neglects government, leaving Martin I ('the Humane', 1396–1410) to stabilise the crown; Martin I's wife Maria de Luna (entry lv7) exercises substantive royal authority during his absences in Sicily; Martin I dies without a legitimate heir in 1410, triggering the succession crisis resolved at Caspe (entry k6) -- the end of the direct Aragonese-Barcelona dynastic line.
entries.note: John I's cultural patronage (French court influence, music): ACA, royal household accounts. Martin I's Sicilian absences and Maria de Luna's government: cross-reference lv7. Martin I's death without heir (30 May 1410): documented -- two years of interregnum before Caspe.

entries.label: Compromise of Caspe — constitutional succession in Aragon
entries.hint: When Martin I of Aragon dies childless in 1410, the Crown of Aragon faces civil war over succession — instead of fighting, representatives of Aragon, Catalonia and Valencia appoint nine arbitrators who meet at Caspe; on 28 June 1412 they select Ferdinand I of Antequera (a Castilian Trastámara) as king, bringing the Trastámara dynasty to both Castile and Aragon, directly enabling the Ferdinand/Isabella marriage of 1469.
entries.note: The Caspe proceedings 1412: Archivo de la Corona d'Aragó — the arbitrators' records. The selection of Ferdinand I: documented. Vicens Vives (1953), Juan II de Aragón: the standard study of the Caspe compromise's dynastic consequences. The direct chain: Caspe (1412) → Trastámaras in Aragon → John II → Ferdinand → marriage to Isabella (1469) → Catholic Monarchs. Without Caspe there is no Ferdinand and Isabella.

entries.label: Ferdinand and Isabella — the Catholic Monarchs
entries.hint: Ferdinand of Aragon and Isabella of Castile marry in 1469 — their union doesn't formally merge the two kingdoms but creates a personal union that acts as one; they complete the Reconquista (1492), expel the Jews (1492), and send Columbus (1492); they are the effective founders of unified Spain.
entries.note: The marriage contract 18 Oct 1469: Archivo de la Corona de Aragón — the primary document of the dynastic union. Their joint titulature ('los Reyes Católicos'): conferred by Pope Alexander VI 1496 — Vatican Archives. The Alhambra Decree (31 Mar 1492): AGS. Columbus commission: AGS Patronato Real. Edwards (2000), Ferdinand and Isabella: the standard English biography.

entries.label: Juan I of Castile -- Aljubarrota and the Portuguese border
entries.hint: Juan I pursues a claim to the Portuguese throne through his wife (daughter of Fernando I of Portugal), leading to the catastrophic defeat at Aljubarrota (1385) -- where Portuguese forces with English archers destroy his army, permanently ending Castilian ambitions in Portugal and establishing Portuguese independence for centuries; his reign also sees the first major anti-Jewish pogroms of 1391 (entry lv4) begin just after his death.
entries.note: Battle of Aljubarrota 14 Aug 1385: documented in Portuguese and Castilian chronicles. The Portuguese independence its confirms: the foundation of the Aviz dynasty, directly cross-referenced to the broader Atlantic context (entry ex3, Canary Islands).

entries.label: Enrique III 'the Infirm' -- consolidation despite illness
entries.hint: Enrique III, chronically ill, nonetheless restores royal authority after the noble disorder of the 1380s and 1390s -- suppressing overmighty magnates, reorganising the royal council, and dispatching the first Castilian embassy to Tamerlane (1403, Ruy Gonzalez de Clavijo's embassy, documented in a remarkable travel account); his early death at 27 leaves a child heir (Juan II) and another regency crisis.
entries.note: Clavijo embassy to Tamerlane (1403): Gonzalez de Clavijo, Embajada a Tamorlan -- extant primary account, BNE. Royal council reorganisation: documented in Castilian administrative records.

entries.label: Juan II of Castile -- the reign of Alvaro de Luna
entries.hint: Juan II reigns for 48 years but is dominated for most of them by his favourite Alvaro de Luna (constable and virtual ruler 1420–1453) -- a case study in the valimiento system that the Habsburgs later institutionalise; Juan II is more interested in poetry than governance (he patronises the Cancionero de Baena, a major literary anthology); de Luna's eventual arrest and execution in 1453 marks the high-water mark of 15th-century Castilian noble power over the crown.
entries.note: Cancionero de Baena (compiled under Juan II's patronage, c.1430): BNE -- primary literary source. Alvaro de Luna's arrest and execution 1453: AHN. The valimiento parallel (cross-reference spain-1516-1700 entries k5/Lerma): documented analytical connection in modern historiography.

entries.label: Enrique IV 'the Impotent' -- the succession crisis that produces Isabella
entries.hint: Enrique IV's reign is the most destabilised of the Trastamara period -- noble factions dispute the legitimacy of his daughter Juana ('la Beltraneja'), stage the 'Farce of Avila' (1465, a theatrical deposition of his effigy), and force him to recognise his half-sister Isabella as heir (Pact of the Bulls of Guisando, 1468); his death in 1474 without resolving the succession triggers the War of Castilian Succession (entry c5) between Isabella and Juana backed by Portugal.
entries.note: The Farce of Avila (5 Jun 1465): documented in chronicle sources -- a theatrical dethronement of Enrique's effigy, one of medieval Castile's most striking acts of noble defiance. Pact of the Bulls of Guisando 19 Sep 1468: AHN -- the agreement naming Isabella heir. Direct cross-reference to c5 (War of Castilian Succession) and lv1 (Isabella I).
keywords: Pact of the Bulls of Guisando

entries.label: Ferdinand I of Aragon -- the first Trastamara king
entries.hint: Ferdinand of Antequera, elected at Caspe (entry k6) as the Castilian Trastamara candidate, becomes Ferdinand I of Aragon -- uniting the Trastamara dynasty across both crowns for the first time; his four-year reign is too short for major policy but his election establishes the dynastic link that eventually produces Ferdinand II (the Catholic), making Caspe the critical hinge between this folio's beginning and its end.
entries.note: Ferdinand I's election at Caspe 28 Jun 1412: ACA -- the Compromise of Caspe (entry k6) documents this. His Castilian identity and the Trastamara connection: directly cross-referenced to k4 (Ferdinand II), his grandson.

entries.label: Alfonso V 'the Magnanimous' -- Naples and the Italian Renaissance
entries.hint: Alfonso V conquers the Kingdom of Naples (1443) after a 15-year campaign, establishing himself there permanently and becoming one of the great Renaissance patrons -- his Neapolitan court hosts Lorenzo Valla, Pontano and other humanists; in his 42-year reign Aragon is largely governed by his brother John from Barcelona while Alfonso pursues Italian glory; his cultural investment in Naples establishes the Italian humanist contacts that eventually feed into Castilian Renaissance culture.
entries.note: Conquest of Naples 2 Jun 1443: ACA and Neapolitan sources. Alfonso V's humanist court (Lorenzo Valla's service, Pontano): Neapolitan royal household records. Ryder (1990), Alfonso the Magnanimous: the standard English biography. His absence from Iberia for the last 15 years of his reign (governing through his brother John): ACA.

entries.label: John II of Aragon -- the father who fought his own son
entries.hint: John II's reign is dominated by his conflict with his own son (Carlos of Viana, heir of Navarre, whose death in 1461 triggers the Catalan Civil War, entry c4) and the prolonged war with Catalonia (1462–1472); he nonetheless survives, suppresses the Catalans, and arranges the marriage of his son Ferdinand to Isabella of Castile in 1469 -- arguably the most consequential royal match in Spanish history -- dying at 82 having outlasted all his enemies.
entries.note: Carlos of Viana's death 23 Sep 1461 and its role triggering the Catalan Civil War: ACA, and documented in the contemporary Dietari de la Generalitat de Catalunya. The 1469 Ferdinand-Isabella marriage (arranged by John II): cross-reference k4. John II's survival of the Catalan war and his death 1479: ACA.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Final reconquest, civil wars and Aragon's Mediterranean campaigns

entries.label: Ferdinand III conquers Córdoba and Seville
entries.hint: Ferdinand III's reconquest of the Guadalquivir valley: on the night of 7/8 January 1236, a small group of Christian knights seize only the eastern suburb (Ajarquía) of Córdoba in a daring coup de main; after months of siege the main city formally surrenders and Ferdinand III enters on 29 June 1236 (the feast of Saints Peter and Paul); Seville falls after a two-year siege on 23 November 1248.
entries.note: The night raid on the Ajarquía suburb 7/8 Jan 1236: Primera Crónica General (Alfonso X). The formal fall of Córdoba 29 Jun 1236: documented in the Primera Crónica — the feast of Saints Peter and Paul. The fall of Seville 23 Nov 1248: ibid. Ferdinand III's multilingual epitaph: Cathedral of Seville. González (1980), Fernando III el Santo: the standard modern biography. The distinction between the January raid and the June formal surrender is confirmed in all primary sources.

entries.label: James I conquers Valencia -- Aragon's own Mediterranean strategy
entries.hint: James I of Aragon takes Valencia from its Muslim rulers in 1238 -- not as a 'consequence' of the Castilian-led victory at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212, fought 26 years earlier and far to the south), but as the culmination of Aragon's OWN, independently-conceived Mediterranean campaign, following his earlier conquest of Mallorca (1229); Valencia becomes the most populous city of the Crown of Aragon and receives its own charter (Furs de Valencia), establishing the third major polity of the Aragonese confederation alongside Aragon proper and Catalonia -- the structural divergence from centralised Castile (entry k1) begins here.
entries.note: James I's Valencia campaign (1232-1238): Llibre dels Fets (James I's own chronicle, one of the earliest royal autobiographies in Europe) -- primary, extant. The Mallorca conquest 1229 (immediately preceding): documented in the same chronicle. Furs de Valencia (1238, granted as a distinct legal code from Aragon's and Catalonia's): Archivo del Reino de Valencia. Burns (1973), Islam Under the Crusaders: Colonial Survival in the Thirteenth-Century Kingdom of Valencia: the standard English study of the Valencia conquest's independent character from Castile's Andalusian campaigns -- directly supporting the structural-divergence framing (cross-reference entry to be added on Castile/Aragon divergence).

entries.label: Sicilian Vespers (1282) -- the founding of Aragon's Mediterranean empire
entries.hint: A popular uprising against Angevin (French) rule in Palermo on Easter Monday 1282 -- the Sicilian Vespers -- massacres the French garrison and invites Peter III of Aragon (whose wife Constance was a Hohenstaufen heir to Sicily) to take the crown; Aragon's acceptance triggers a war with the Angevins and the Papacy that establishes Aragonese Sicily as a permanent Mediterranean possession (entry w3) -- the single event that transforms Aragon from an Iberian kingdom into a Mediterranean maritime power.
entries.note: The Sicilian Vespers 30 Mar 1282: documented in contemporary Sicilian and papal sources, and in the Cronaca of Bartolomeo di Neocastro (a near-contemporary eyewitness account). Peter III's acceptance of the Sicilian crown (Aug 1282) and the resulting 'War of the Sicilian Vespers' (1282-1302, ending at the Peace of Caltabellotta): Archivo de la Corona de Aragon. Runciman (1958), The Sicilian Vespers: the standard English study. Direct connection to entry w3 (Catalan-Aragonese empire): this event is its founding moment.

entries.label: Battle of Rio Salado (1340) -- the last Marinid invasion repelled
entries.hint: Alfonso XI of Castile, with Portuguese support, decisively defeats a combined Marinid (Moroccan) and Nasrid (Granadan) army at the Rio Salado -- the last serious attempt by a North African power to reverse the Reconquista through direct invasion; the victory secures the Strait of Gibraltar for Christian naval power and removes the threat of large-scale Moroccan intervention in Iberia for the remainder of the medieval period, leaving Granada to face Castile alone for the next 150 years until 1492 (entry c-Granada).
entries.note: Battle of Rio Salado 30 Oct 1340: Cronica de Alfonso XI -- primary chronicle. Portuguese participation under Afonso IV: Portuguese chronicle sources confirm the joint Iberian Christian effort. O'Callaghan (2011), The Gibraltar Crusade: Castile and the Battle for the Strait: the standard modern study, framing this as the decisive end of Marinid intervention. The 150-year gap to the Granada War (1482-1492, entry c-Granada): the direct long-term consequence -- Granada is left isolated and besieged, but the lack of external support delays rather than accelerates its final fall.

entries.label: War of the Two Peters (1356-1369) -- Castile vs Aragon, with England and France
entries.hint: Pedro I of Castile ('the Cruel') and Pedro IV of Aragon ('the Ceremonious') fight a 13-year war that devastates the Aragonese-Valencian borderlands -- both sides draw in foreign mercenary companies released by lulls in the Hundred Years' War, with England backing Pedro I of Castile and France (via Henry of Trastamara, entry k7) backing the Aragonese side; the war merges directly into the Castilian Civil War (entry c3) when Henry of Trastamara, fighting alongside Aragon, turns the conflict into a Castilian succession war -- ending with Pedro I's death at Montiel (1369) and the Trastamara takeover of Castile.
entries.note: The war's destructiveness to the Aragonese-Valencian frontier (depopulation of border towns): documented in Aragonese royal correspondence, Archivo de la Corona de Aragon. English support for Pedro I of Castile (the Black Prince's involvement at Najera, 1367, technically a separate but connected campaign): English chronicle sources (Froissart). French/free-company support for Henry of Trastamara via Aragon: Cronica del rey don Pedro (Ayala). The merger with the Castilian Civil War (c3) at Montiel 1369: the same battle ends both conflicts. Ferrer i Mallol (1987): the standard modern study connecting this war to the broader Hundred Years' War.
keywords: Pedro I of Castile the Cruel
Pedro IV of Aragon the Ceremonious

entries.label: Castilian civil war — Pedro I vs Enrique II
entries.hint: The Castilian civil war between King Pedro I ('the Cruel') and his illegitimate half-brother Enrique de Trastámara involves England (Black Prince supports Pedro), France (Du Guesclin supports Enrique) and the Crown of Aragon; Pedro is murdered at Montiel (1369); the Trastámara dynasty takes power.
entries.note: The murder of Pedro I at Montiel 23 Mar 1369: documented in multiple chronicles. The Black Prince's intervention (Battle of Nájera 1367): English chronicles document his role. Froissart's Chronicles: detailed account of the English involvement — BnF. The Trastámara dynasty's subsequent rule: documented in royal archives. Russell (1955), The English Intervention in Spain and Portugal: the standard study.

entries.label: Catalan Civil War — the crisis that forced the Ferdinand-Isabella marriage
entries.loc: Catalonia (Generalitat vs John II of Aragon)
entries.hint: John II of Aragon fights a decade-long civil war against the Catalan Generalitat — the war devastates Catalonia's economy and breaks Barcelona's mercantile dominance; John II's desperation for a Castilian alliance to end the war is the direct political driver of the Ferdinand-Isabella marriage (1469), which he arranges.
entries.note: The Catalan Civil War: Vicens Vives (1953), Juan II de Aragón — the standard study. The Generalitat's war against John II: Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó. The connection to the Ferdinand-Isabella marriage: explicit in Vicens Vives — John II needed Castilian support and arranged the marriage as a strategic alliance. The Barcelona economy's collapse: documented in municipal records (Arxiu Municipal de Barcelona). Catalonia does not recover its pre-1462 commercial dominance until the 18th century.

entries.label: Castilian War of Succession — Isabella vs Joanna la Beltraneja
entries.hint: Isabella's accession in 1474 triggers a civil war — her niece Joanna ('la Beltraneja') is backed by Afonso V of Portugal; the Battle of Toro (1476) turns the conflict; the Treaty of Alcaçovas (1479) secures Isabella's crown and, crucially, divides Atlantic maritime spheres — Portugal keeps Africa and the Atlantic islands; Castile gets the Canaries; this shapes the age of exploration.
entries.note: The Battle of Toro 1 Mar 1476: documented in chronicles. Treaty of Alcaçovas 4 Sep 1479: AGS Patronato Real and Arquivo Nacional da Torre do Tombo (Lisbon) — primary texts. The Atlantic sphere division: Article 2 grants Portugal exclusive rights to navigate the African coast; Castile gets the Canaries. Suárez Fernández (1964), Política internacional de Isabel la Católica Vol.I: the standard study. The treaty directly shapes the framework within which Columbus will negotiate his 1492 commission.

entries.label: The Granada War (1482-1492) -- the final conquest
entries.loc: Kingdom of Granada (Ronda, Malaga, Granada city)
entries.hint: Ferdinand and Isabella's decade-long war against the Nasrid Kingdom of Granada -- exploiting a Nasrid succession crisis (the rivalry between Boabdil and his uncle al-Zagal) -- combines siege warfare against fortified cities (Ronda 1485, Malaga 1487, Baza 1489) with the systematic use of gunpowder artillery on a scale not previously seen in Iberia; the war ends with the surrender of Granada city on 2 January 1492, negotiated through the Capitulations of Granada (which initially guarantee Mudejar religious and legal rights, later broken); it is the culmination of nearly eight centuries of Christian-Muslim territorial conflict in the peninsula and is funded substantially through Castilian and Aragonese crusade taxation (the bula de cruzada).
entries.note: The Capitulations of Granada (Nov 1491): Archivo General de Simancas -- the terms initially guaranteeing Mudejar rights (cross-reference entry on Mudejares), later systematically violated leading to the 1499-1501 forced conversions. The siege of Malaga (1487) and its harsh terms (the entire population enslaved or expelled, unlike most other sieges): documented in Castilian chronicle sources. Gunpowder artillery use: documented in royal accounts (AGS) -- among the earliest large-scale siege-artillery campaigns in Europe. Ladero Quesada (1967), Castilla y la conquista del reino de Granada: the standard modern study using the financial and military records of the campaign. The bula de cruzada funding: papal bulls of 1482, 1485, 1489 -- AGS Patronato Real.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: The Siete Partidas, Cortes and the development of Iberian law

entries.label: Cortes of Castile and Aragon — early representative assemblies
entries.hint: Within Castile and Aragon's own institutions (the 1188 Cortes of Leon predates this folio and belonged to a separate kingdom), both crowns develop powerful representative assemblies through the 13th-14th centuries -- Castile's Cortes (regularly summoned from Alfonso X onward) and the Aragonese Corts, which by 1283 (entry o4) achieves the strongest constitutional check on royal power anywhere in medieval Europe; both develop documentary traditions of petitions and royal responses that remain primary sources for medieval representative government.
entries.note: Castilian Cortes summons under Alfonso X and successors: Archivo General de Simancas, Cortes records. The Aragonese Corts' constitutional strength (cross-reference entry o4, the General Privilege of 1283): Gonzalez Anton (1975), Las Cortes de Aragon. Leon's 1188 Cortes (often cited as Europe's earliest, predating Magna Carta by 27 years) is the documented PRECEDENT for this tradition but belongs to pre-union Leon, outside this folio's 1230 start -- O'Callaghan (1989), The Cortes of Castile-Leon 1188-1350 discusses the institutional continuity from Leon's precedent into the united Castile-Leon Cortes after 1230.

entries.label: Siete Partidas — composed by Alfonso X, enforced by Alfonso XI
entries.hint: Alfonso X compiles the Siete Partidas (c.1256–1265) — the most comprehensive medieval Iberian law code; but it is never promulgated in his lifetime due to fierce noble opposition favouring local fueros; it remains a theoretical compendium until 1348 when his great-grandson Alfonso XI gives it statutory force in the Ordenamiento de Alcalá.
entries.note: Siete Partidas (c.1256–1265): BNE holds the primary manuscripts. The failure to promulgate under Alfonso X: documented in the political conflict with the Castilian nobility. The Ordenamiento de Alcalá 1348 (Alfonso XI): gives the Siete Partidas legal force — text in AHN. US influence: still referenced in Louisiana courts until 1825. Burns (2001), Las Siete Partidas (Pennsylvania): the standard English translation. The gap between composition and enforcement is itself a primary fact about the limits of royal legislative power in 13th-century Castile.

entries.label: Aragonese pactismo — constitutional limits on royal power
entries.hint: The Crown of Aragon is defined by pactismo — the legal theory that royal power is strictly limited by mutual contracts sworn between the king and the regional estates (Corts) of Catalonia, Aragon and Valencia; the General Privilege of Aragon (1283) and the institution of the Justicia Mayor institutionalise this constitutional limitation; it offers a powerful alternative to Castilian absolutism.
entries.note: The General Privilege of Aragon (Privilegio General, 1283): Archivo de la Corona d'Aragó — the foundational pactismo document. The Justicia Mayor of Aragon: Archivo de la Diputación del Reino de Aragón. The Catalan Constitutions (Usatges of Barcelona, 12th c. onwards): Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó. Lalinde Abadía (1980), Los fueros de Aragón: the standard study. Elliott (2018), Scots and Catalans: places Aragonese pactismo in European comparative perspective. The contrast with Castilian absolutism: the Cortes' different powers documented comparatively in Procter (1980), Curia and Cortes in León and Castile.

entries.label: Santa Hermandad (1476) -- royal policing and the new state
entries.hint: Isabella's Cortes of Madrigal (1476) reorganises the medieval municipal hermandades (peace leagues) into the Santa Hermandad -- a permanent, crown-funded police and judicial force with summary jurisdiction over rural crime, banditry and disorder; funded by a direct levy on towns, it represents one of the Catholic Monarchs' most effective instruments of state centralisation, providing security for trade routes and royal authority in the countryside independent of noble jurisdiction -- a direct institutional ancestor of later royal policing across the Habsburg empire.
entries.note: Santa Hermandad founding, Cortes of Madrigal 1476: AGS, Consejo Real -- the founding ordinances survive. Lunenfeld (1970), The Council of the Santa Hermandad: A Study of the Pacification Forces of Ferdinand and Isabella: the standard English study. The funding mechanism (a per-household levy, the 'hermandad tax'): documented in municipal tax records across Castile. The institution's effective lifespan (largely superseded by the 1490s as royal authority consolidates, though formally continuing): Lunenfeld discusses its self-limiting design -- a temporary instrument that succeeded so well it made itself less necessary.

entries.label: Spanish Inquisition established — 1478
entries.hint: Ferdinand and Isabella petition Pope Sixtus IV to establish a royal Inquisition — the papal bull of 1 November 1478 authorises it; the Inquisition is under royal (not papal) control; its first auto de fe is held in Seville in 1481; Tomás de Torquemada is appointed Inquisitor General in 1483.
entries.note: Papal bull Exigit sincerae devotionis (1 Nov 1478): Vatican Archives. Torquemada's appointment 1483: documented. The first auto de fe (Seville, 6 Feb 1481): documented in contemporary records. AHN Inquisición: the primary archive for all subsequent proceedings. Kamen (1997), The Spanish Inquisition: the standard modern study — covers this founding period.

entries.label: Sentencia Arbitral de Guadalupe (1486) -- ending the mals usos
entries.hint: Ferdinand II, acting as arbitrator after the Remensa Wars (entry lv5), issues the Sentencia Arbitral de Guadalupe -- permanently abolishing the six 'mals usos' (bad customs) that had bound Catalan peasants to the land, taxed their marriages and inheritance, and permitted lords to physically punish them; in exchange, peasants pay a one-time compensation (51,000 lliures) to their lords. The sentence establishes a new legal and agrarian order for the Catalan countryside that endures for centuries -- one of the few late-medieval peasant movements in Europe to achieve a durable legal victory rather than violent suppression.
entries.note: Sentencia Arbitral de Guadalupe 21 Apr 1486: Archivo de la Corona de Aragon -- the full text survives. The six mals usos (intestia, cugucia, arsia, firma de espoli forzada, exorquia, ius maletractandi) individually enumerated and abolished: documented in the sentence's text. The 51,000-lliura compensation: AGI/ACA financial records. Freedman (1991), The Origins of Peasant Servitude in Medieval Catalonia: the standard English study, framing Guadalupe as a rare durable peasant legal victory in European context. Direct cross-reference to lv5 (Remensas): the Sentencia is the legal resolution of that conflict.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Church, Inquisition and the end of religious pluralism

entries.label: Las Huelgas — royal monastery and Cistercian centre
entries.hint: Founded by Alfonso VIII in 1187 -- just before this folio's start -- Las Huelgas becomes the royal pantheon of Castile throughout the 1230-1492 period, with kings and queens of every reign documented in this folio (Ferdinand III, Alfonso X, Alfonso XI and successors) buried within its walls; a Cistercian intellectual centre holding an extraordinary archive including the Almohad battle standard captured at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212), its music manuscripts are primary sources for 13th-century polyphony continuously produced through the folio's timeframe.
entries.note: Las Huelgas burial records spanning 1230-1492 (Ferdinand III's immediate predecessors through the Trastamara kings): documented in the monastery's own archive, Archivo del Monasterio de Santa Maria la Real de Las Huelgas, Burgos -- extant. The Codex Las Huelgas (polyphonic music manuscript, c.1300-1325): falls within this folio's period despite the monastery's 1187 founding. The Almohad standard (captured 1212, displayed at Las Huelgas): documented, extant. The institution's founding (1187) is the origin point, but its FUNCTION as Castile's royal pantheon is continuously exercised throughout 1230-1492 -- the same 'continued relevance' pattern as Tordesillas (cross-reference spain-1516-1700, entry ex3).

entries.label: Samuel Halevi Abulafia — treasurer, synagogue builder
entries.hint: Samuel Halevi Abulafia, treasurer of Pedro I of Castile, builds the El Tránsito synagogue in Toledo (c.1357) — the finest medieval synagogue in Spain; its Hebrew inscriptions praise both God and Pedro I; its survival after the 1391 pogroms and the 1492 expulsion is itself a primary source.
entries.note: El Tránsito synagogue (c.1357): extant, Toledo (Museo Sefardí). The Hebrew inscriptions: translated and documented. Samuel Halevi's subsequent arrest and execution by Pedro I (c.1360): documented in chronicles. Baer (1961), A History of the Jews in Christian Spain Vol.II. The synagogue's conversion to a church after 1492 and its survival: documented. The inscription praising Pedro I alongside God: primary evidence for Jewish-Christian political collaboration.

entries.label: Vincent Ferrer and Pablo de Santa Maria -- the converso theological crisis
entries.hint: Following the 1391 pogroms (entry lv4), two figures define the theological aftermath from opposite sides of the same conversion: Vincent Ferrer, a Dominican preacher whose apocalyptic sermons across Castile and Aragon (1411-1412) drive mass conversions through fear -- directly preceding the Disputation of Tortosa (1413-1414) -- while Solomon ha-Levi, a rabbi who converts in 1390 and becomes Pablo de Santa Maria, Bishop of Burgos, produces sophisticated converso theology (the Scrutinium Scripturarum) arguing Christianity's truth from within rabbinic learning; together they represent the two faces of the converso phenomenon -- coercive pressure and genuine (or strategic) intellectual accommodation -- that together produce the unstable mass-converso population the Inquisition (entry o3) is later established to police.
entries.note: Vincent Ferrer's sermons and the Disputation of Tortosa (1413-1414): documented in contemporary Hebrew and Latin accounts (Shlomo ibn Verga's later compilation draws on eyewitness material). Ferrer canonised 1455 -- Vatican records. Pablo de Santa Maria's Scrutinium Scripturarum (completed by his son Alonso de Cartagena, also Bishop of Burgos): BNE manuscripts -- a foundational converso theological text. Roth (1995), Conversos, Inquisition, and the Expulsion of the Jews from Spain: the standard study connecting both figures to the structural converso crisis that precedes and explains the 1478 Inquisition (entry o3).

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Gothic cathedrals, the Cantigas and Mudéjar art

entries.label: Gothic cathedrals — Burgos, Toledo and León
entries.hint: The great Gothic cathedrals of Burgos, Toledo and León — all begun in the 13th century under French influence — are the monumental primary sources of medieval Spanish religious expression; León's cathedral (c.1,800 sq metres of stained glass) is called 'the house of light'; Burgos and Toledo develop into the hyper-ornate Isabelline Gothic by the late 15th century.
entries.note: Burgos Cathedral (begun 1221): UNESCO 1984 — building accounts in Archivo de la Catedral de Burgos. Toledo Cathedral (begun 1226): extant — Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo. León Cathedral (begun c.1205): extant — c.1,800 sq metres of medieval stained glass, the largest concentration in a Romanesque/Gothic structure in Europe. Williams (1977), A Spanish Romanesque: contextualises the transition. The Isabelline Gothic (Flamboyant-Mudéjar fusion): San Juan de los Reyes, Toledo (1476–1504) — commissioned by the Catholic Monarchs, extant.

entries.label: Mudéjar architecture — Muslim craftsmen for Christian patrons
entries.hint: Mudéjar architecture is produced by Muslim craftsmen working for Christian and Jewish patrons — it fuses Islamic geometric decoration, horseshoe arches and brick construction with Gothic and Romanesque structural forms; it is uniquely Iberian, found nowhere else.
entries.note: UNESCO World Heritage: Mudéjar architecture of Aragon (1986, extended 2001) — towers of Teruel and Zaragoza. The Seville Alcázar (14th c.): extant (UNESCO) — built by Pedro I using Nasrid craftsmen from Granada; the primary monument. The Sinagoga del Tránsito, Toledo: also Mudéjar. Dodds (1990), Architecture and Ideology: the standard study of Mudéjar's cross-cultural meaning.

entries.label: Cantigas de Santa María — Alfonso X's illustrated songbook
entries.hint: The Cantigas de Santa María is 420 poems with musical notation — the largest collection of medieval monophonic songs from any single source; four illuminated manuscripts survive with miniatures documenting 13th-century daily life; the musical notation is the primary source for Iberian medieval music.
entries.note: Four manuscripts: El Escorial T.I.1 (the Florence codex), El Escorial B.I.2, BNE 10069, BnF Esp.361. Katz & Keller (1987), Studies on the Cantigas de Santa María: the standard scholarly collection. The miniatures as social history: Ferreira (2019) uses them as primary sources for musical instruments, clothing and daily life. The musical notation: enables modern performance — documented in recordings.

entries.label: Castilian vernacular poetry -- Juan Ruiz to Jorge Manrique
entries.hint: Juan Ruiz's Libro de Buen Amor (c.1330-1343) -- a sprawling, ironic first-person narrative mixing erotic adventure, moral allegory and folk tale in the cuaderna via metre -- is Castilian literature's first great vernacular masterpiece, establishing a tradition of self-aware, formally inventive poetry; this tradition culminates a century and a half later in Jorge Manrique's Coplas por la muerte de su padre (1476) -- a meditation on mortality and fame ('Que se hizo el rey don Juan?') that becomes the most memorised poem in the Spanish language, its stately pie quebrado stanzas studied in Spanish schools to this day.
entries.note: Libro de Buen Amor: three manuscript copies survive (Toledo, Salamanca, Gayoso), BNE -- the Toledo manuscript is the earliest, c.1389 copy of an earlier original. Coplas por la muerte de su padre (1476, written for Manrique's father Rodrigo Manrique, Master of the Order of Santiago): BNE, multiple early printed editions from the 1490s onward. Lida de Malkiel (1950s) and subsequent scholarship: establish both works' canonical status. The continuous classroom presence of the Coplas in Spanish education: documented in curriculum records into the present.
keywords: Juan Ruiz Libro de Buen Amor

entries.label: The Llibre Vermell de Montserrat — pilgrimage songs (c.1399)
entries.hint: The Llibre Vermell (Red Book) of Montserrat contains 10 songs for pilgrims to sing or dance at the shrine — the earliest documented use of dancing in a Christian devotional context; the manuscript also contains plague songs written during the Black Death epidemics; it is the oldest extant Catalan music manuscript.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Llibre Vermell de Montserrat (c.1399): Arxiu de l'Abadia de Montserrat. The 10 songs include polyphonic and monophonic pieces. Gómez (1990), La música medieval en España: the standard study. The dancing at shrines: the text explicitly describes pilgrims dancing in the nave — 'because the pilgrims are accustomed to watch at night in the church'. The plague songs: composed c.1348–1350 — documented by internal references.
keywords: Llibre Vermell Red Book of Montserrat

entries.label: Ausias March -- the first major poet writing in Catalan/Valencian
entries.hint: Ausias March abandons the troubadour convention of writing love poetry in Occitan -- the prestige literary language of earlier Catalan-Aragonese courts -- and writes instead in his own Valencian/Catalan vernacular, producing intensely introspective, psychologically complex love and philosophical poetry that influences Castilian poets (including, eventually, the Petrarchan tradition's Spanish reception); his decision to write in the vernacular is itself a literary-political statement, asserting Valencian/Catalan as a language of high culture independent of both Occitan convention and Castilian dominance -- a linguistic parallel to the Crown of Aragon's institutional separateness (entry o4).
entries.note: Ausias March's Cants (poems): multiple manuscript copies survive, Biblioteca de Catalunya and Biblioteca Nacional de Espana. His abandonment of Occitan for Valencian: a documented and remarked-upon literary choice in contemporary commentary. His influence on later Castilian Petrarchism (via 16th-century translations and imitations): documented in literary-historical scholarship (Pages, 1912-1914, the foundational critical edition and study). The linguistic-political dimension: directly parallels the institutional pactisme documented in entry o4 -- language and constitution as parallel assertions of Aragonese-Valencian distinctiveness from Castile.

entries.label: International Gothic painting -- Dalmau, Martorell and Huguet
entries.hint: Lluis Dalmau, sent by Alfonso V to study with Jan van Eyck in Flanders, returns to paint the Mare de Deu dels Consellers (1443-1445, Barcelona) -- a work combining Flemish oil-painting realism (minutely observed faces, fabric, light) with Italian compositional elegance; Bernat Martorell and later Jaume Huguet continue this synthesis in altarpieces across Catalonia and Valencia, producing the peak of pictorial expression in the Crown of Aragon -- a distinctly Mediterranean variant of International Gothic that predates and parallels developments in Castile (where Fernando Gallego works in a related Flemish-influenced idiom from the 1460s).
entries.note: Dalmau's Mare de Deu dels Consellers (1443-1445): Museu Nacional d'Art de Catalunya, extant -- commissioned by Barcelona's city council, payment records survive (Arxiu Historic de la Ciutat de Barcelona). Dalmau's documented trip to Flanders (1431, in Alfonso V's retinue) and contact with van Eyck's workshop: Archivo de la Corona de Aragon. Martorell's Sant Jordi altarpiece: Art Institute of Chicago and other fragments, extant. Huguet's Sant Antoni Abat altarpiece (Barcelona Cathedral): extant. Post (1930-1966), A History of Spanish Painting (14 vols): the foundational comprehensive study covering all these painters and Fernando Gallego.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Alfonso X's scriptorium and the intellectual synthesis

entries.label: Ramon Llull — missionary philosophy and the Art
entries.hint: Ramon Llull is the first European philosopher to write systematic philosophy in a vernacular language (Catalan) — his Ars Magna proposes a logical method for generating theological arguments; he learns Arabic and makes missionary journeys to North Africa; the tradition that he was stoned to death in Tunis is a later hagiographical legend; most scholars conclude he died of old age at sea or in Mallorca c.1315–1316.
entries.note: Ars Magna (c.1274): Latin and Catalan manuscripts — BnF and Archivo de la Corona d'Aragó. Llull's Catalan works: the first philosophical corpus in Catalan. Bonner (2007), The Art and Logic of Ramon Llull: the standard English study. His death: most critical scholarship concludes he died at sea or in Mallorca c.1315–1316 of natural causes; the stoning-at-Tunis story develops in later Franciscan hagiography. His influence on later logic: Leibniz credited him — documented.

entries.label: Archbishop Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada — De Rebus Hispaniae
entries.hint: Archbishop Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada's De Rebus Hispaniae (On the Affairs of Spain, c.1243) is the first comprehensive history of the Iberian Peninsula — he was present at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212) and his account of it is an eyewitness document; the work defines the Reconquista as a providential national narrative.
entries.note: De Rebus Hispaniae (c.1243): manuscripts in multiple archives; printed edition Lorenzana (1793). Rodrigo's eyewitness account of Las Navas de Tolosa: the most detailed contemporary narrative. Linehan (1993), History and the Historians of Medieval Spain: the standard critical study of the source tradition. The work's 'national narrative' framing: Linehan argues it retrospectively constructs a Spanish identity.
keywords: Archbishop Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada De Rebus Hispaniae On the Affairs of Spain

entries.label: Alfonsine Tables — astronomy in three languages
entries.hint: Alfonso X commissions revised astronomical tables — the Alfonsine Tables — from a team of Jewish and Muslim astronomers working in Toledo; compiled in Arabic, translated to Castilian, later to Latin; used by European astronomers including Copernicus until the 17th century.
entries.note: Alfonsine Tables (c.1270): compiled by Isaac ibn Sid and Judah ben Moses ha-Cohen. The Paris edition 1483: the standard Latin version. Copernicus used them: documented in his notes. Chabás & Goldstein (2003), The Alfonsine Tables of Toledo: the standard modern study. The collaboration of Jewish and Muslim astronomers under a Christian king: a primary example of convivencia in intellectual production. This Iberian astronomical tradition continues for over two centuries: Abraham Zacuto, working in Salamanca until the 1492 expulsion (after which he relocates to Portugal), produces the Almanach Perpetuum (1496) -- tables and an improved metal astrolabe that Portuguese navigators, including Vasco da Gama's pilots, use directly for oceanic navigation, making the Alfonsine-to-Zacuto lineage a direct technical bridge from 13th-century Toledo astronomy to the Age of Discovery.

entries.label: The Toledo Translation School under Alfonso X -- Aristotle into Latin
entries.hint: Under Alfonso X's direct patronage, the Toledo translation workshops reach their intellectual peak in the mid-13th century -- teams of Christian, Jewish and Muslim scholars translate Aristotle and his great Arabic commentators (Averroes, Avicenna, al-Farabi) from Arabic into Latin (often via an intermediate Castilian vernacular stage, itself a major innovation); these translations become the textual basis for European Scholasticism -- Thomas Aquinas's engagement with Aristotle is mediated substantially through Toledo's output -- making this the single most consequential intellectual transfer of medieval Iberia, surpassing even the more famous 12th-century translation activity in scale and direct philosophical impact.
entries.note: Alfonso X's translation patronage (distinct from and exceeding the earlier 12th-century Archbishop Raymond-era translations): documented in the colophons of surviving manuscripts naming Alfonso's translators (Yehuda ben Moshe, among others). The Castilian-vernacular intermediate stage (a genuine Alfonsine innovation -- translating Arabic to Castilian, then Castilian to Latin): d'Alverny (1982), 'Translations and Translators', in Renaissance and Renewal in the Twelfth Century -- though focused on the 12th century, contextualises the 13th-century Alfonsine continuation. Aquinas's documented use of these Latin Averroes/Aristotle translations: established in modern Aquinas scholarship (Dod, 1982, 'Aristoteles latinus'). This entry directly complements t1 (Alfonsine Tables) and t3 (De Rebus Hispaniae) as evidence of Alfonso X's broader intellectual programme.

entries.label: Nebrija's Gramatica de la lengua castellana (1492) -- language as empire
entries.hint: Antonio de Nebrija publishes the first grammar of any modern European vernacular language in the same year as the fall of Granada and Columbus's voyage -- in his prologue, addressed to Isabella, Nebrija explicitly argues that language has always followed empire ('siempre la lengua fue companera del imperio') and offers Castilian grammar as a tool for governing newly incorporated and soon-to-be-discovered peoples; whether or not Isabella grasped the prologue's full implications in 1492, the book's framing -- standardised language as an instrument of imperial administration -- proves prophetic for the following century of American conquest.
entries.note: Gramatica de la lengua castellana, dated 18 Aug 1492 in its colophon: BNE, multiple extant early editions. The prologue's 'language follows empire' argument: the original text, widely quoted in modern scholarship (Lleal 1992 critical edition). Nebrija's earlier Latin grammar (1481) establishes his methodology before applying it to Castilian. The 1492 publication date's coincidence with Granada's fall (Jan 1492, entry c9) and Columbus's departure (Aug 1492): three events of the same year, explicitly discussed as a thematic cluster in modern historiography (cross-reference w1, '1492 -- the year Spain makes the modern world').

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Wool, silver and the economy of medieval Castile

entries.label: The Mesta — Castilian wool and the transhumance economy
entries.hint: Alfonso X charters the Mesta (shepherds' guild) in 1273 — the guild organises the seasonal migration of up to 2 million merino sheep; but the Crown grants the Mesta immense grazing monopolies and transit rights (cañadas reales) over farmland in exchange for immediate royal loans; this stifles food agriculture, creates chronic grain deficits in Castile, and lays the structural seeds of long-term agricultural stagnation.
entries.note: Mesta charter 1273: AGS and AHN. Klein (1920), The Mesta: A Study in Spanish Economic History 1273–1836: the standard study. The merino sheep numbers: documented in 14th-c. fiscal records. The Castile-Flanders wool trade: documented in Bruges merchant archives and Castilian royal customs records. The Mesta's long-term impact on Castilian agriculture: documented in subsequent land-use surveys. The negative fiscal consequences: Klein (1920), The Mesta ch.5 — documents how the cañadas reales legally overrode enclosed farmland. The Castilian grain deficit: documented in 15th-century price series. The immediate royal loans in exchange for monopoly rights: AGS Contadurías. The connection to Spain's later agricultural problems: Ringrose (1983), Madrid and the Spanish Economy 1560–1850 traces the Mesta's long shadow.

entries.label: Black Death in Iberia — 1348 and the Jewish pogroms
entries.loc: Catalonia; Castile; Aragon (arrival via Mediterranean ports)
entries.hint: The Black Death reaches Iberia via Catalan ports in 1348 — killing an estimated 30–40% of the population; Jews are blamed for poisoning wells and massacred (Tàrrega 1348; Barcelona 1348); the pogroms of 1348 foreshadow and directly cause the larger pogroms of 1391.
entries.note: The Tàrrega pogrom 1348: documented in contemporary municipal records and now genetically confirmed — MDPI study (March 2026) using DNA from six mass graves at the Roquetes necropolis, Tàrrega. The Barcelona pogrom 1348: documented in municipal archives (Arxiu Municipal de Barcelona). Nirenberg (1996), Communities of Violence: the standard study of 14th-century Iberian pogroms. The Black Death mortality: Benedictow (2004), The Black Death 1346–1353, estimates 30–40% for Iberia.

entries.label: Castilian monetary debasement -- from the Black Death to civil war
entries.hint: In the wake of the Black Death (entry s2) and the dynastic civil war (entry c3), Castilian kings repeatedly debase the maravedi -- reducing its silver content to fund military campaigns and noble payoffs (the mercedes enriquenas, entry k7) -- producing runaway inflation that erodes fixed incomes and intensifies agrarian discontent; this monetary instability becomes endemic to Castilian fiscal practice for the rest of the medieval period, a structural pattern that resurfaces with even greater severity in the Habsburg vellon crisis three centuries later (cross-reference spain-1516-1700, entry s3).
entries.note: Maravedi debasement records under Pedro I, Henry II and successors: AGS, Casa de la Moneda. Ladero Quesada (1973), Historia de Sevilla: La ciudad medieval, and (1993), La Hacienda Real de Castilla en el siglo XV: the standard studies of Castilian fiscal practice and currency debasement in this period. The connection between mercedes enriquenas (k7) and fiscal strain: debasement is documented as the direct means of funding these noble grants. The structural parallel to the later vellon crisis (1516-1700, s3): both represent crown debasement as a substitute for sustainable taxation, a recurring pattern in Castilian fiscal history.

entries.label: Taula de Canvi (1401) -- Barcelona's early public bank
entries.hint: Barcelona's city council establishes the Taula de Canvi (literally 'exchange table') in 1401 -- a municipal deposit and exchange institution guaranteeing deposits with the city's own credit, designed to restore confidence after a wave of private banker failures; it is among the earliest public banks in Europe, predating the Bank of Amsterdam (1609) by two centuries, and provides a model of municipal financial guarantee that influences later European public banking.
entries.note: Taula de Canvi founding ordinance 1401: Arxiu Historic de la Ciutat de Barcelona -- extant. Usher (1943), The Early History of Deposit Banking in Mediterranean Europe: the standard English study identifying the Taula as among Europe's earliest public banks. The private banking failures preceding its creation (Barcelona bankers' collapses in the 1380s-90s): documented in the same Arxiu records. The institution's longevity (operating in some form into the 19th century): documented in Catalan financial-historical scholarship.

entries.label: Barcelona's commercial decline and Valencia's golden age
entries.hint: The Catalan Civil War (entry c4, 1462-1472) devastates Barcelona's commercial position -- plague, depopulation and the war's disruption of trade combine with longer-term Genoese and Castilian competition to end Barcelona's medieval Mediterranean primacy; in the same decades, Valencia -- benefiting from its silk trade with Granada and Italy (entry ex5), its own banking institutions, and relative insulation from the Catalan conflict -- experiences its 'segle d'or' (golden century), becoming the Crown of Aragon's most dynamic commercial centre by 1492, a shift in economic gravity within the same composite monarchy.
entries.note: Barcelona's population and trade volume decline post-1462: documented in Arxiu Historic de la Ciutat de Barcelona customs and demographic records. Carrere (1967), Barcelone, centre economique a l'epoque des difficultes 1380-1462: the standard study of Barcelona's decline. Valencia's rise documented in Furs records and the Lonja construction (entry ex5, begun 1482) as physical evidence of commercial confidence at exactly the moment Barcelona contracts. Cross-reference to entry c4: the war is the proximate cause of Barcelona's collapse.

entries.label: Paper manufacture at Xativa -- Europe's documentary revolution
entries.date: 1244 (Xativa's conquest); paper mills active through 15th c.
entries.hint: Xativa, conquered by James I in 1244, had been one of al-Andalus's paper-manufacturing centres -- among the first in Europe, using techniques transmitted via the Islamic world from China -- and continues production under Christian rule, supplying paper for the Crown of Aragon's notarial culture, the Cortes' documentary traditions (entry o2) and the Consolat de Mar's maritime contracts (entry ex4); cheap, locally-produced paper (versus imported parchment) is a precondition for the explosion of administrative documentation -- royal registers, notarial protocols, merchant ledgers -- that makes the Crown of Aragon's archives (Archivo de la Corona de Aragon) among medieval Europe's most complete.
entries.note: Xativa's paper production under Islamic rule (pre-1244): documented in Arabic geographical sources (al-Idrisi). Continuity under Christian rule: paper mill references in Valencian municipal and royal documents from the late 13th century onward, Archivo del Reino de Valencia. Hunter (1947), Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft: the standard study tracing the China-Islamic world-Iberia-Europe transmission route via Xativa. The connection to ACA's archival completeness (a recurring evidentiary basis for this entire folio's entries): explicitly noted in ACA's own institutional history.

entries.label: Basque ferrerias -- hydropowered ironworking for European export
entries.hint: The ferrerias (ironworks) of the Basque Country use water-powered hammers and bellows -- harnessing the region's abundant streams and high-quality iron ore -- to produce iron and finished goods (anchors, weapons, agricultural tools, ship fittings) on a scale that makes the Basque coast one of medieval Europe's major iron-exporting regions, shipping to England, Flanders and across the Atlantic-facing trade; this industrial base directly underpins the Basque shipbuilding tradition (entry te4) and provides much of the iron for Castilian and Aragonese military equipment throughout the period covered by this folio.
entries.note: Ferreria sites and production records: documented in municipal and seigneurial archives across Biscay and Guipuzcoa (Archivo Historico de Bizkaia). Export volumes to England (iron and finished goods via Bilbao and other Basque ports): English customs records (TNA) document Basque iron imports from the 14th century. Diez de Salazar (1984), Ferrerias en Guipuzcoa: the standard regional study. The connection to shipbuilding (te4): Basque shipyards drew directly on local iron for fittings, anchors and tools -- a regional industrial cluster.

entries.label: The Majorcan cartographic school -- Abraham Cresques and the Catalan Atlas
entries.hint: Abraham Cresques, a Jewish cartographer in Palma, produces the Catalan Atlas (1375) for the future Charles V of France -- combining accurate Mediterranean portolan-chart coastlines (drawing on the lateen-rigged ship navigation that made such precision possible) with detailed, often first-hand information on West Africa, including the earliest European depiction of a named West African ruler (Mansa Musa of Mali, shown holding a gold nugget); Majorca's position as a Jewish-Christian-Muslim commercial crossroads makes it medieval Europe's pre-eminent centre of cartographic knowledge synthesis.
entries.note: Catalan Atlas (1375): Bibliotheque nationale de France, extant -- one of the most studied medieval maps in existence. Cresques's identity and Jewish cartographic workshop in Palma: documented in Majorcan archival records (Arxiu del Regne de Mallorca), and in his expulsion from Majorca in 1391 (forced conversion, taking the name Jaume Ribes) -- connecting this entry to entry r3/lv2's converso themes. The Mansa Musa depiction: art-historical analysis of the Atlas itself. Pinto (2017), Medieval Portolan Charts as Cartographic Renaissance: contextualises the Majorcan school's broader output beyond this single famous example.

entries.label: The caravel -- the ship that enables Atlantic exploration
entries.hint: The caravel -- a small, highly manoeuvrable ship combining lateen sails (for sailing close to the wind, an Islamic-Mediterranean technology) with a shallow draft suited to exploring unknown coastlines and rivers -- emerges from Andalusian and Portuguese Atlantic shipyards by the mid-15th century; its combination of speed, manoeuvrability and relatively small crew requirements makes it the vessel of choice for exploratory voyages along the African coast and across the Atlantic -- two of Columbus's three ships in 1492 (the Pinta and Nina) are caravels, while the larger Santa Maria (a carrack) carries the bulk cargo; the caravel's design lineage feeds directly into the larger galleon (cross-reference spain-1516-1700, entry te4).
entries.note: Caravel design and the lateen-sail/shallow-draft combination: Casado Soto (1988), Los barcos espanoles del siglo XVI -- traces the design lineage from caravel to galleon. The Pinta and Nina as caravels, Santa Maria as a carrack: documented in Columbus's own log (Diario de a bordo) and subsequent ship-construction scholarship. Andalusian Atlantic shipyards (Huelva, Palos): the documented origin of Columbus's 1492 vessels. Phillips & Phillips (1992), The Worlds of Christopher Columbus: discusses the ships' construction and the broader Iberian Atlantic shipbuilding context. Direct lineage to galleon (te4 in spain-1516-1700): explicit cross-folio technological continuity.

entries.label: Consolat de Mar — the founding code of Mediterranean maritime law
entries.hint: The Consolat de Mar (Consulate of the Sea) is the pioneering maritime law code compiled and consolidated in Barcelona and Valencia across the second half of the 13th century -- governing merchant shipping, naval insurance, freight contracts and trade disputes across the entire Mediterranean basin; adopted voluntarily by merchant communities from Genoa to Ragusa and later by Venice, it becomes the most influential medieval legal export in European commercial history -- a body of customary law that outlives the Crown of Aragon itself.
entries.note: Consolat de Mar: earliest dated manuscript c.1282 (Valencia); compiled versions from Barcelona. Archivo de la Corona d'Aragó holds the primary manuscripts. First printed edition Valencia 1494: BNE. Pene Vidari (1979), Il Consolato del Mare: the standard modern study. The code's Mediterranean adoption: documented in Genoese, Venetian and Pisan merchant archives. The code's influence: still cited in Spanish and Italian maritime law into the 19th century — documented.
keywords: Consolat de Mar Consulate of the Sea

entries.label: Iberian-Genoese alliance — trade and Columbus's context
entries.hint: Genoese merchants are embedded in Castilian and Aragonese trade networks throughout the 14th and 15th centuries — providing credit, maritime expertise and commercial connections; Christopher Columbus is a Genoese working for Castile; his voyage is the product of this long Ibero-Genoese commercial partnership.
entries.note: Genoese merchant records in Seville: Archivo Histórico Provincial de Sevilla. The Genoese colony in Seville: documented from the 13th century. Columbus's Genoese origins: documented in notarial records from Genoa. Phillips (1988), Columbus: the standard biography, documents the Genoese-Castilian commercial world. The connection between the Genoese network and the 1492 voyage: Fernández-Armesto (1991), Columbus.

entries.label: Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean empire — the Catalan Atlas (1375)
entries.hint: The Catalan Atlas (1375) made by Abraham Cresques in Mallorca is the most detailed world map of the 14th century — showing the Silk Road, West African gold kingdoms, and the Indian Ocean; it is the product of the Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean mercantile intelligence.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Catalan Atlas (1375): BnF (Manuscrit espagnol 30) — digitised. Made by Abraham Cresques, a Jewish cartographer in Mallorca. The detail of West Africa (including Mali's gold): drawn from traveller accounts. Pujades (2007), Les cartes portolanes: the standard study. The Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean trade documentation: Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó — extensive merchant records.

entries.label: Conquest of the Canary Islands — Spain's colonial blueprint
entries.loc: Canary Islands (Gran Canaria; Tenerife; La Palma)
entries.hint: Castile conquers the indigenous Guanches of the Canary Islands between 1402 and 1496 — the methods of conquest, systematic subjugation, forced conversion and land division are the direct administrative and military blueprint applied to the Americas from 1492 onwards; the Canaries are Spain's first colonial experiment.
entries.note: The Canary conquest: begun by Jean de Béthencourt for Castile 1402 — documented. The later royal campaigns (Gran Canaria 1478–1483; La Palma 1492–1493; Tenerife 1494–1496): AGS. The Guanche extermination and assimilation: documented in colonial records (AGS and Archivo Municipal de Las Palmas). Fernández-Armesto (1982), The Canary Islands after the Conquest: the standard study. The blueprint argument: Verlinden (1970), The Beginnings of Modern Colonization: explicitly connects Canary methods to American practice.

entries.label: Lonja de la Seda -- Valencia's silk exchange and the Granada-Italy network
entries.hint: Construction begins in 1482 on Valencia's Lonja de la Seda (Silk Exchange) -- a monumental Gothic merchant hall whose spiralling stone columns and vaulted trading floor physically embody Valencia's role as the hub of a silk network spanning Nasrid Granada (the principal raw-silk producer in Iberia), the Crown of Aragon's own Mediterranean shipping (entry ex1), and Italian banking houses; the building's commission in the very years of the Granada War (entry c9) is a striking juxtaposition -- Christian Valencia's commercial prosperity depends on silk imported from the Muslim kingdom its sister crown is simultaneously conquering.
entries.note: Lonja de la Seda construction begun 1482 (completed in stages to 1548): Arxiu Municipal de Valencia, building accounts -- extant building, UNESCO World Heritage. Granada's raw silk exports to Valencia: documented in Valencian customs records (Archivo del Reino de Valencia) showing substantial silk imports from Nasrid territory throughout the 15th century, continuing even during the Granada War. Igual Luis (1998), Valencia e Italia en el siglo XV: documents the Italian banking connections. The juxtaposition with c9 (Granada War, same years): an explicit point of analytical interest in modern economic history of the conquest period.
keywords: Valencia Lonja de la Seda Silk Exchange

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: The Catholic Monarchs and the birth of the global world

entries.label: The Catalan-Aragonese empire — Sicily, Naples, Athens
entries.loc: Sicily; Sardinia; Naples; Duchy of Athens (Greece)
entries.hint: The Crown of Aragon builds a vast Mediterranean maritime empire — Sicily (1282, via the Sicilian Vespers), Sardinia (1323), Naples (1442); the mercenary Almogavar army seizes the Duchy of Athens and Neopatria in Greece (1311–1388); at its peak Aragon controls the western and central Mediterranean, fundamentally altering the balance of power in the eastern Mediterranean.
entries.note: The Sicilian Vespers (30 Mar 1282): documented in multiple contemporary chronicles. The Almogavar conquest of Athens: Chronicle of Ramon Muntaner (primary — Muntaner was present). The Duchy of Athens under Catalan rule 1311–1388: documented in the Archive of the Crown of Aragon. Setton (1948), Catalan Domination of Athens: the standard study. Hillgarth (1976), The Spanish Kingdoms 1250–1516 Vol.I: the standard English survey. The Almogavar mercenaries: documented as the most feared military force in early 14th-century Europe.

entries.label: Almogavars and the Catalan Company — Athens and beyond
entries.hint: After the Sicilian wars, the Catalan Company (almogavar mercenaries) takes service with Byzantium, turns on their employers, conquers the Duchy of Athens (1311) and Neopatria; the Crown of Aragon rules duchies in Greece for 77 years — the most extraordinary geopolitical overreach of medieval Iberia, fundamentally altering the eastern Mediterranean balance of power.
entries.note: Ramon Muntaner, Crònica (c.1325): primary source — eyewitness account of the Catalan Company by a participant. The Battle of the Cephissus 15 Mar 1311: documented in Muntaner and in Byzantine sources. The Duchy of Athens under Aragonese rule 1311–1388: Archivo de la Corona d'Aragó — administrative records. Setton (1948), Catalan Domination of Athens: the standard study. The almogavars: professional light infantry, documented in Muntaner and in military records.

entries.label: Treaties of Torrellas and Elche (1304-1305) -- the Castile-Valencia border
entries.hint: The treaties of Torrellas (1304) and Elche (1305), mediated by Pope Boniface VIII, resolve a long dispute over the Kingdom of Murcia between Castile and the Crown of Aragon -- partitioning Murcia, with Aragon gaining Alicante, Orihuela and the southern Valencian territories (permanently extending the Kingdom of Valencia's southern border) while Castile retains Murcia city and the bulk of the kingdom; this settlement establishes the Castile-Valencia border that endures, essentially unchanged, into the modern administrative map of Spain -- a rare medieval treaty whose geographic outcome remains directly legible today.
entries.note: Treaty of Torrellas 8 Aug 1304 and Treaty of Elche 19 May 1305: Archivo de la Corona de Aragon and Archivo Municipal de Murcia -- both texts survive. Papal mediation (Boniface VIII): documented in papal registers. The border's modern persistence (the Alicante/Murcia provincial boundary closely follows the 1305 line): a point made in Spanish historical geography (Torres Fontes, the standard regional historian of medieval Murcia). This is a border-SETTING treaty rather than a war-ending one -- no single documented Conflict entry corresponds to the dispute it resolves, distinguishing it from the Peace of Utrecht/Pyrenees pattern (cross-reference spain-1700-1808 and spain-1516-1700).

entries.label: 1492 — the year Spain makes the modern world
entries.loc: Granada; Palos de Moguer; Atlantic Ocean
entries.hint: Three events in 1492 authorised by Ferdinand and Isabella from the Alhambra: Granada falls (2 January), Jews expelled (31 March), Columbus departs (3 August); the same year Nebrija publishes the first Castilian grammar — the tools of empire, religious homogeneity and overseas expansion all coincide.
entries.note: Granada's fall 2 Jan 1492: documented. Alhambra Decree 31 Mar 1492: AGS. Columbus's departure 3 Aug 1492: his journal (BNF). Nebrija's Gramática castellana (1492): BNE — first grammar of a modern European vernacular language. Elliot (2002), Imperial Spain 1469–1716: the standard modern study of the Catholic Monarchs' legacy. The connection between language standardisation and empire: Nebrija's preface explicitly argues grammar is the 'companion of empire'.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: Mudejares -- Muslims living under Christian rule
entries.hint: As Castile and Aragon conquer the great agricultural valleys of the Guadalquivir, Ebro and Jucar, millions of Muslims -- the Mudejares -- remain in their homes under Christian lordship rather than emigrating; in Valencia they form the majority of the rural population well into the 15th century, retaining their faith, Arabic language (developing Aljamiado, Romance written in Arabic script, for religious texts), legal status under their own qadis for internal matters, and -- critically -- the irrigation systems (acequias) and agricultural techniques developed under earlier Islamic rule, which Mudejar farmers continue to operate and maintain, sustaining the huertas that remain the economic backbone of Valencian agriculture; the Capitulations of Granada (entry c9) initially extend similar guarantees to Granada's population, before their systematic violation after 1499.
entries.note: Mudejar population proportions in Valencia (majority rural population into the 15th century): Boswell (1977), The Royal Treasure: Muslim Communities under the Crown of Aragon -- the standard English study, using royal and seigneurial tax records (Archivo del Reino de Valencia). Aljamiado manuscripts: BNE and other Spanish libraries hold substantial Aljamiado collections from this period. Acequia maintenance by Mudejar communities: documented in irrigation-tribunal records (the Tribunal de las Aguas de Valencia, itself of Islamic-era origin, continues functioning under Mudejar and later Christian administration -- still operating today, one of Europe's oldest continuously functioning courts). Glick (1970), Irrigation and Society in Medieval Valencia: connects the technological legacy directly to Mudejar lived experience.

entries.label: African slavery in medieval Iberia — the Valencian slave market
entries.hint: The Crown of Aragon operates slave markets in Valencia and Barcelona from the 14th century — trading primarily in sub-Saharan Africans, Moors, and enslaved people from the Black Sea region; the Valencian notarial records are among the best-documented medieval European slave trade archives.
entries.note: Valencian notarial records documenting slave sales: Arxiu del Regne de València — published in Verlinden (1955), L'Esclavage dans l'Europe médiévale Vol.I. The Barcelona slave market: documented in Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó. Blumenthal (2009), Enemies and Familiars: Slavery and Mastery in Fifteenth-Century Valencia: the standard modern study using the notarial records. The scale: Blumenthal documents 10,000+ slave sales in Valencia alone 1375–1425.

entries.label: The conversos — forced converts and their impossible position
entries.hint: The conversos (Jewish converts to Christianity, mostly forced) are the central social problem of 15th-century Castile — too Christian to be Jewish, too recently Jewish to be trusted as Christians; the Inquisition is created specifically to investigate their sincerity; the blood purity statutes (limpieza de sangre) later bar them from office.
entries.note: The converso population: Beinart (2002) estimates 100,000+ forced conversions in 1391. The Inquisition's primary targets: AHN Inquisición records show conversos as the dominant category in 15th-c. proceedings. The Toledo blood purity statute (1449): the first such statute — documented in AHN (see Visigothic Spain folio connection). Netanyahu (1995), The Origins of the Inquisition: the foundational study of the converso problem. This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT: the creation of a new intermediate category (converso) that satisfies neither Christian nor Jewish frameworks.

entries.label: Pogroms of 1391 — the turning point for Iberian Jews
entries.hint: A wave of pogroms sweeps the Christian kingdoms of the Iberian Peninsula in 1391 — beginning in Seville in June and spreading across Castile and Aragon; these cities had been under Christian rule for over a century; thousands killed, many more forced to convert; the converso class created directly causes the Inquisition's founding and the 1492 expulsion.
entries.note: The Seville pogrom June 1391: documented in municipal records (Archivo Municipal de Sevilla). All cities affected (Seville, Córdoba, Toledo, Valencia, Barcelona) were under Christian rule — this entry belongs in the Christian kingdoms folio, not al-Andalus. Baer (1961), A History of the Jews in Christian Spain Vol.II. The Tàrrega 1348 mass graves: MDPI genetic study (March 2026). The forced conversions' scale: Beinart (2002) estimates 100,000+ converted in 1391.

entries.label: Maria de Luna -- Aragon's capable queen-lieutenant
