entries.hint: Maria de Luna, queen consort of Martin I of Aragon, serves repeatedly as her husband's lieutenant-general -- governing the Crown of Aragon's mainland territories during his absences in Sicily, issuing decrees in her own name, managing the kingdom's response to plague outbreaks, and mediating between the Crown and Catalonia's Cortes; her correspondence and administrative output (over 6,000 surviving letters in her registers) make her one of the best-documented female rulers of medieval Europe, exercising substantive executive authority that goes well beyond the ceremonial queenship typical of the period -- a striking parallel, a century early, to Isabella I's later co-rule (entry lv1).
entries.note: Maria de Luna's registers (over 6,000 letters, 1396-1406): Archivo de la Corona de Aragon, Cancilleria Real -- extant and extensively catalogued. Garcia Herrero & Munoz Fernandez (2003 and subsequent), editions and studies of Maria de Luna's correspondence: the modern scholarly basis. Her management of plague response in Catalonia: documented within the same register collection. Earenfight (2010), The King's Other Body: Maria de Luna and the Royal Authority in Medieval Aragon: the standard English-language study, explicitly arguing for her substantive (not merely ceremonial) exercise of royal authority.

entries.label: Isabel de Villena -- abbess and author of the Vita Christi
entries.hint: Isabel de Villena, abbess of Valencia's Convent de la Trinitat for nearly four decades, writes the Vita Christi (printed posthumously 1497) -- a life of Christ written in Valencian prose for the nuns under her care, notable for its sustained focus on female biblical figures (Mary, Mary Magdalene, the women at the tomb) given expanded voice and psychological interiority rarely found in male-authored devotional literature of the period; she is among the earliest identifiable female authors writing in any Iberian vernacular, and her convent's royal patronage (she was an illegitimate granddaughter of a Trastamara prince) gave her work unusual circulation and prestige for a woman's text.
entries.note: Vita Christi, printed Valencia 1497 (posthumous): extant early printed editions, Biblioteca Nacional de Espana and Biblioteca Valenciana. Isabel de Villena's royal lineage (illegitimate granddaughter of Martin I's brother, or of Ferdinand I depending on the genealogy followed) and abbacy (1462-1490): documented in convent and royal records. Hauf (1990s), critical studies of the Vita Christi's distinctive female-centred theology: the modern scholarly basis. This entry pairs with a7 (Ausias March) as Valencian-language literary production, and with lv1 (Isabella I) and lv7 (Maria de Luna) as the folio's cluster of documented female agency.

entries.label: Remensas peasants — the largest successful peasant revolt in late medieval Europe
entries.hint: Catalan peasants (pagesos de remença) live under the 'Bad Customs' (mals usos) — brutal feudal abuses binding them to the land, taxing their marriages, and permitting physical punishment; their Remensa Wars (1462–1486) are the largest successful peasant rebellion in late medieval Europe; Ferdinand II abolishes the Bad Customs in the Sentencia Arbitral de Guadalupe (1486).
entries.note: The mals usos documentation: Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó — the six Bad Customs listed in contemporary documents. The Remensa Wars: Vicens Vives (1945), El gran sindicat remença: the standard study. The Sentencia Arbitral de Guadalupe 21 Apr 1486: Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó — Ferdinand II's arbitration abolishing the mals usos. Freedman (1991), The Origins of Peasant Servitude in Medieval Catalonia: the standard English study. This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT: the legal abolition of inherited servitude for Catalan peasants — the only such wholesale peasant liberation in 15th-century Iberia.

entries.label: Isabella I — queen regnant and architect of Spain
entries.hint: Isabella I of Castile is crowned in Segovia on 13 December 1474 — ruling in her own right; she initiates the Inquisition, completes the Reconquista, expels the Jews, and authorises Columbus; she is the most consequential woman in the founding of the modern world and the primary political actor behind every 1492 event.
entries.note: Isabella's coronation 13 Dec 1474: Segovia — documented in contemporary chronicles. Her personal correspondence: AGS — documented. Liss (1992), Isabel the Queen: the standard modern biography. The Granada capitulations and Alhambra Decree: both issued jointly but Isabella's role documented as dominant in Spanish sources. The contrast with her husband: Ferdinand handled Aragon; Isabella handled Castile — documented in their separate chancelleries.
